negative_experience.situation: When a patient presents with fever, weight loss, and poor appetite without specific localizing symptoms, and initial history and lab review yield no evidence, the doctor should avoid premature diagnosis of malignancy without imaging or targeted investigation.

arguments.question: Can you describe the nature of the right-sided facial pain? Is it constant or intermittent, and does anything trigger or relieve it?

clinical_turn.patient_or_tool_response: {'question': 'The pain on the right side of my face started as sharp, stabbing attacks that lasted minutes to hours, with periods of relief in between. But over time, it became constant and severe — there’s no real pain-free time now. The pain starts on the inner part of my nose and radiates across my cheek and upper gum toward my ear. It’s much worse when I clench my jaw or chew — the pain increases immediately. Placing a towel over my right side of the face helps relieve it slightly. I don’t know any specific triggers that make it worse, and I don’t have eye tearing or redness anymore, though I did have them earlier.'}

current_turn_information: The pain on the right side of my face started as sharp, stabbing attacks that lasted minutes to hours, with periods of relief in between.
But over time, it became constant and severe — there’s no real pain-free time now.
The pain starts on the inner part of my nose and radiates across my cheek and upper gum toward my ear.
It’s much worse when I clench my jaw or chew — the pain increases immediately.
Placing a towel over my right side of the face helps relieve it slightly.
I don’t know any specific triggers that make it worse, and I don’t have eye tearing or redness anymore, though I did have them earlier.
case_memory.prior_information_summary: 79-year-old female, history of trigeminal neuralgia, SDH/SAH, macular degeneration, osteopetrosis, nasal polyps, migraines. Presented with 1-month history of severe right-sided facial pain, initially episodic, now constant. Pain radiates from inner nose to cheek/upper gum/ear, sharp stabbing, worsened by jaw clenching/chewing. Tegretol titrated, Medrol dose pack ineffective. CTA showed right-sided vessel compressing trigeminal nerve root. Neurosurgery consulted; transferred to neurology for pain management. Denies neurological deficits. Vitals: BP 158/92, SaO2 96. Neuro exam: intact cranial nerves (except mild v3 decrease right), no focal deficits. Final result: Anomalies of cerebrovascular system.

case_memory.prior_information_summary: 79-year-old female with right-sided facial pain, history of trigeminal neuralgia, osteopetrosis, nasal polyps, and migraines.
